title: There are fewer people without electricity, even as the global population grows
source: Energy Sector Management Assistance Program (ESMAP). SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset.
note: Regional estimates of population without access to electricity are calculated by World Bank staff.
visdescription: Chart decomposing global access to electricity by regions, then zooming in on Sub-Saharan Africa, which it decomposes by country.
keyFactLabel: of the 666 million people without access to electricity live in Sub-Saharan Africa.

scenes.text: While the global population has increased by almost 2 billion people since 2000…
… the number of people without electricity halved, falling from 1.3 billion in 2000 to 666 million in 2023.

All regions, except Sub-Saharan Africa, contributed to this rapid decline, particularly South Asia. In 2000, both South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa accounted for roughly the same share of the global population without electricity. But by 2023, South Asia achieved near-universal access, while the number of people without electricity in Sub-Saharan Africa increased. Despite significant progress in access in the region, it was not enough to keep up with population growth.
In 2023, Sub-Saharan Africa was home to 581 million people without electricity. This translates to nearly 9 in 10 of the world’s people living without electricity.
And just eight countries account for half the world’s people who still lack access to electricity.
Over one-third of the global population without electricity live in the region’s most populous nations: [emphasis: Nigeria], the [emphasis: Democratic Republic of Congo], and [emphasis: Ethiopia].
